coredata.dc:title: Ultra-broadband triple-peak white light-emitting carbonized polymer dots materials based on Förster resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Jing
Cheng, Dengke
Gu, Hailing
Yang, Daiqi
Xiao, Chi
Li, Yi
Zhao, Hongjia
Liu, Longtao
Lu, Chunyi
Li, Qijun
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with white-light-emitting property have greatly promising application in next generation of lighting and display technologies. However, most of the reported CPD materials exhibit single peak emission and narrow emission band, resulting in difficulty to obtain white-light emission. In this work, the ultra-broadband triple-peak emission (red, green, and blue) CD-based materials with high-efficiency white light-emitting property are realized for the first time by the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Blue and green emissions are derived from the fluorescence and phosphorescence of donor CPDs, while the red light is derived from receptor by FRET. Remarkably, the fabricated CPD materials show bright pure white-light emission with high overall quantum yield (QY) of 36% and the full width at half maximum of 235 nm. Besides, afterglow colors of CPD-based materials can be tuned from green to red by adjusting the ratio of donor and acceptor. Based on the high-efficiency and wide-spectrum pure white light emission characteristics of CPD-based composites, white light emitting diodes were fabricated and they exhibit bright warm white light with CIE and CCT of (0.35, 0.31) and 4041 K.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Resonance energy transfer
White LEDs
Multicolor afterglow
Ultrabroad-band

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CPDs, and the inset is the corresponding size distribution. (b) FTIR spectra of CPDs and CPD-RhB@BA. (c) High‐resolution B 1s XPS spectrum of CPD-RhB@BA.

(a) Absorption and phosphorescence spectra of the CPD@BA; absorption and fluorescence spectra of RhB. (b) Fluorescence spectra of the CPD@BA and CPD-RhB@BA composite. (c) Phosphorescence decay curves of the CPD@BA and CPD-RhB@BA composite (λex = 395 nm and λem = 530 nm). (d) CIE photo of CPD@BA and CPD-RhB@BA composite.

(a) Schematic diagram of the prerequisites for the FRET process to occur between donor (CPDs) and acceptor (RhB). (b) formation mechanism of efficient and ultrabroad-band white light emission for CPD-RhB@BA-1 (Ex.: Excite; Fluor.: Fluorescence; Phos.: Phosphorescence).

(a) Phosphorescence spectra and (b) lifetime of CPDs and RhB composites with different RhB contents under 395 nm excitation. (c) Plot of FRET efficiency and I600/I530 changes of the CPDs and RhB composites as a function of RhB concentration. (d) CIE 1931 coordinates of CPDs and RhB composites with different RhB contents under 395 nm excitation.

(a) A digital photo of a white LED light illuminating the words “Jiangsu University”; (b) EL spectrum of the WLEDs with InGaN UV (395 nm); (c) Calculated PL emission color coordinates in the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. (d) Digital photographs of multicolor LEDs using different CPD-RhB@BA composites as phosphors.
Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure for the CPD-RhB@BA composite.

head.ce:title: Ultra-broadband triple-peak white light-emitting carbonized polymer dots materials based on Förster resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Science and Technology, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225009, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with white-light-emitting property have greatly promising application in next generation of lighting and display technologies. However, most of the reported CPD materials exhibit single peak emission and narrow emission band, resulting in difficulty to obtain white-light emission. In this work, the ultra-broadband triple-peak emission (red, green, and blue) CD-based materials with high-efficiency white light-emitting property are realized for the first time by the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Blue and green emissions are derived from the fluorescence and phosphorescence of donor CPDs, while the red light is derived from receptor by FRET. Remarkably, the fabricated CPD materials show bright pure white-light emission with high overall quantum yield (QY) of 36% and the full width at half maximum of 235 nm. Besides, afterglow colors of CPD-based materials can be tuned from green to red by adjusting the ratio of donor and acceptor. Based on the high-efficiency and wide-spectrum pure white light emission characteristics of CPD-based composites, white light emitting diodes were fabricated and they exhibit bright warm white light with CIE and CCT of (0.35, 0.31) and 4041 K.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the fourth-generation light source, white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) are widely used in display and lighting applications because of their low energy consumption, high luminous efficiency and long lifetime [1–5]. As a light conversion layer, phosphor absorbs the energy of external photons and converts them into white light emission, which plays a crucial role in the luminous performance of WLEDs. Currently, developed WLEDs are usually mixed with phosphors of different luminescent colors to achieve white light, which is difficult to adjust the ratio of phosphors, leading to problems such as easy phase separation, self-absorption, and poor light stability [6–8]. Compared with multi-components combination, single-component white light emissive materials effectively avoid uncoordinated phosphor mixing, thus simplifying the device preparation steps and improving the luminescence performance of the device [9–11]. However, traditional single-phase phosphors such as rare-earth materials and semiconductor quantum dots have problems of high energy consumption or toxicity [12], so the development of environmentally friendly and low-cost single-component white light-emitting phosphors are essential to advance the research of WLEDs.

In recent years, due to the excellent properties of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), such as wide luminescence spectrum, low synthesis cost, strong light stability and low toxicity [13–33], single-component white light-emitting materials based on CPDs with fluorescence/phosphorescence emission have been developed and applied to WLEDs [34,35]. For example, Wang and coworkers [36] prepared white-light-emissive CPDs based on fluorescence/phosphorescence components cooperatively, and quantum yield (QY) of ≈41% for the white light emission. However, the reported white emissive CPDs relatively narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) due to the poor phosphorescence emission. Recently, our group [37] reported a prominent fluorescent/phosphorescent dual-emission white CD-based material with extraordinarily high phosphorescence QY of 46% and a broad full width at half maximum over 180 nm. However, the phosphorescence emission peak is typically located at shorter wavelengths (530 nm) region and the intensity of the bimodal emission is not tunable, resulting in relatively bluish white light emission. Therefore, it is important to develop a single-component ultra-broadband white light-emitting material with sufficient red color components.

Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative energy transfer from an excited state donor to a ground state acceptor [38–42]. It mediated by electron dipole-dipole coupling consistent with the premises of the spectral overlap of the energy donor-acceptor system (D-A). By regulating the distance between the donor and acceptor, the relative intensity of the donor and acceptor can be effectively regulated. Choosing suitable donors and acceptors, long wavelength phosphorescent emission can be realized. Considering these, constructing white LEDs based on the FRET method may be a very effective approach, but it has never been reported before.

In this work, a new strategy for realizing ultra-wideband and efficient triple-peak white light-emitting CPD materials by FRET is proposed for the first time. In this system, high-efficiency blue fluorescence and green phosphorescent dual emission CPD@BA and RhB with strong red fluorescence emission were chosen as the donor and acceptor of FRET, respectively. In view of the high overlap between the green phosphorescence emission spectrum of CPDs and the absorption spectrum of RhB, CPD-RhB@BA composites with three emission peaks (blue, green and red) were synthesized by confining the CPDs and RhB in a thermal annealed boric acid (BA) matrix. As the content of RhB increases, the green phosphorescence peak of CPD-RhB@BA composites gradually weakens, while the red phosphorescence peak gradually strengthens due to the increase in energy resonance transfer efficiency, which displays tunable multicolor emissions from blue, white to orange. And the highest energy transfer efficiency can reach 99%. Significantly, the QY value of pure white light-emitting CPD-RhB@BA composite is as high as 36%, and the FWHM can reach 235 nm. Based on the high-efficiency and wide-spectrum pure white light emission characteristics of CPD-RhB@BA, WLEDs were fabricated by using an UV-LED chip and they exhibit bright warm white light with CIE and CCT of (0.35, 0.31) and 4041 K.

To achieve efficient white light-emitting CPD materials based on FRET theory, a suitable energy donor and acceptor are necessary. Based on our previous work [43], high-efficiency phosphorescent CPD-based materials (CPD@BA) with green emission (530 nm) were synthesized by confining the CPDs in a thermal annealed BA matrix, which can be used as an energy donor. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the RhB has a strong absorption peak at 525 nm, along with the presence of fluorescence emission centered in the 600 nm red region extending into the near infrared. RhB is selected as an energy acceptor in view of its absorption and emission. As shown in Scheme 1 , the CPDs were synthesized by one‐pot hydrothermal treatment of lycorine hydrochloride at 200 °C for 2 h and then mixed CPDs and RhB in an aqueous solution. Finally, the CPD-RhB@BA composite was synthesized by composited with BA under heat-melt treatment.

In the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 a), the prepared CPDs are quasi-spherical with average diameter of 6 ± 2 nm. The chemical structure and photophysical properties of CPDs and CPD-RhB@BA were investigated. The Fourier infrared (FTIR, Fig. 1b) spectrum shows that the characteristic peaks of CPDs at 1626 and 1606 cm−1 were attributed to strong stretching vibrations of CO and CC [18], and the peak at 1424 cm−1 corresponded to the C–N functional groups. After hydrothermal treatment, new absorption peaks were observed at 927 and 1490 cm−1 belonging to C–B and B–O stretching vibrations [37], respectively, indicating that BA molecules were functionalized on the surface of CPDs after heat treatment. The result of FTIR data were further confirmed by XPS-based structural analysis. The high-resolution (HR) B 1s spectra of CPD-RhB@BA (Fig. 1c) showed three characteristic peaks at 193.8, 192.9, and 192 eV belonging to B–O, B2O3, and BCO2 [44], respectively, which represented the presence of boron oxide and boron-oxygen co-doping in CPDs. All these results indicated that CPDs were chemically bonded in the BA matrix.

The synthesized CPD@BA exhibits bright green emission under a UV lamp (395 nm) and green afterglow after ceasing the UV irradiation, where the afterglow can last for up to 17 s under ambient conditions (Fig. S1). The afterglow emission peak of CPD@BA is centered around 530 nm and an average lifetime of 1.77 s, and the phosphorescence spectra and lifetime decay are illustrated in Fig. S2. As can be seen from the UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 2 a, there are two distinct absorption peaks for CPD@BA aqueous solution, which the peak around 270 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC and the broad peak around 370 nm can be attribute to the n-π* transition of CO or C–N [45–48], respectively. In the PLE spectra of the CPD@BA powders under 530 nm emission (Fig. S3), the excitation bands located in the range from 300 to 500 nm are basically the same as the absorption peaks of CPD@BA solution, indicating that the phosphorescence emission peak at 530 nm mainly originates from the CO or C–N bonds in CPDs.

In order to obtain highly efficient white light emitting CPD-RhB@BA composites based on FRET theory, several samples with different mixing ratios of CPDs and RhB were prepared. The obtained CPD-RhB@BA composite materials were named CPD-RhB@BA-X (X = 1, 2, 3). The detailed process of preparation and experimental parameters is provided in the supporting information. In steady-state photoluminescence (Fig. 2b), the prepared CPD@BA had two clear emission peaks at 430 and 530 nm. The peak at 530 nm is almost consistent with the phosphorescence spectrum. The temperature-dependent phosphorescence spectra of CPD@BA powder (Fig. S4) showed that the intensity of phosphorescence at 530 nm was greatly increased at 77 k compared to 293 K. The peak at 530 nm was confirmed to belong to phosphorescence [37]. After the addition of RhB, the relative intensity of the peaks at 430 and 530 nm decreases gradually, while a new peak appeared at the long wavelength (600 nm). The fluorescence intensity of the samples was compared with or without CPDs doping (Fig. S5). The peak intensity of the prepared CPD-RhB@BA was stronger than RhB@BA at 600 nm, demonstrating the existence of FERT process between CPDs and RhB. In addition, compared the phosphorescence intensity of RhB@BA, BA and CPD@BA (Fig. S6), the phosphorescence intensity of RhB@BA and BA was essentially negligible, indicates that the phosphorescence only comes from CPDs, excluding the influence of RhB and BA's own phosphorescence.

The energy transfer efficiency E between CPD@BA and RhB can be calculated according to the following equation:
E = 1 ‐ I /
I 0
where I and I0 are the donor CPD@BA afterglow intensity in the presence and absence of the acceptor RhB, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2c and Table S1, with the increase of RhB acceptor doping, the phosphorescence lifetime monitored at 530 nm decreases from 1.77 to 1.47 s, which further proves that FRET occurs between the T1 state of donor CPD@BA and the S1 state of acceptor RhB [40]. The S1 state exciton of CPD@BA emits green phosphorescence due to the effect of spin orbit coupling, and some of the carriers are transferred from the S1 energy level to the T1 energy level through the intersystem crossing (ISC) process, which produces a green phosphorescence emitted by the radiative composite of the T1-state exciton, whereas the shorter-wavelength phosphorescence with higher energies is absorbed by the RhB through the FRET process, which emits a long-wavelength afterglow in the red region.

By confining CPDs and RhB in thermally annealed BA matrix, we realized composites with three emission peaks (430, 530 and 600 nm). Among them, the peaks at 430 and 530 nm originate from the fluorescence and phosphorescence of the CPDs, respectively, while the peak at 600 nm is realized by FERT theory. By adjusting the concentration of the acceptor RhB, we have prepared an ultra-broadband triple-emission high-efficiency white light-emitting CPD material. In Fig. 2d, CIE is red-shifted from (0.24, 0.32) of CPD@BA to (0.40, 0.35) of CPD-RhB@BA-3, while The CIE of CPD-RhB@BA-1 reaches (0.33, 0.34), which is very close to the standard white light. In addition, the CPD-RhB@BA-1 composite exhibited the phosphorescence and fluorescence even when placed in a bare environment for more than 30 days (Fig. S7), which indicates its good stability. Significantly, the QY value of pure white light-emitting CPD-RhB@BA-1 is as high as 36%, and the FWHM reaches 235 nm, which is very suitable for preparing white LED as phosphors. In addition, the CPD1 were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of levofloxacin with nitrogen heterocyclic structure, and then complexed with different concentrations of RhB into BA. Under suitable ratios, CPD1-RhB@BA composites with white-light emission could be obtained, similar to that of CPDs prepared from lycorine (Fig. S9).

The realization of the energy resonance transfer process generally requires the fulfillment of several conditions. First, there needs to be sufficient spectral overlap between the donor emission and the acceptor absorption. Second, the distance between the donor and the acceptor needs to be less than 10 nm. Finally, the vibrational frequencies of the donor and acceptor dipoles should remain the same (Fig. 3 a) [40]. A large spectral overlap exists between the phosphorescence of CPD@BA and the absorption of RhB, which is consistent with the premise that the generation of FRET. The distance between donor and acceptor was ensured to be less than 10 nm by confining CPDs and RhB in a thermally annealed BA matrix. Therefore, an effective D-A pair is formed between CPDs and RhB, which ensures efficient energy resonance transfer resulting in red light emission. Based on the above analysis, we propose the formation process of CPD-RhB@BA composite and the source of efficient solid-state luminescence (Fig. 3b). During the hydrothermal process, the polymerization and carbonation processes between hydrochloride and BA produced abundant B, N, and O-containing functional groups. The excess BA crystallizes near CPD to form B2O3 polycrystalline network, which effectively restricts the molecular motion and insulates the air from water and oxygen, thus inhibiting the non-radiative leaps of CPD and stabilizing the triplet state, and enhances the phosphorescence emission located at the wavelength of 530 nm. In addition, the FRET that occurs between CPD@BA and Rh B fulfills the conditions for energy transfer and can be described as follows:
3CPD@BA*(T1) → CPD@BA (S0) + hv
1RhB(S0) + hv → 1RhB*(S1)
1RhB*(S1) → 1RhB(S0) + hv’

where 1RhB*(S1) and 1RhB(S0) are the singly heavy excited state and singly heavy ground state of RhB; while 3CPD@BA*(T1) and CPD@BA(S0) represent the triplet excited state and the singlet ground state of CPD@BA, respectively; h is Planck's constant, v and v’ represent photons with different emission frequencies. The CPD@BA absorbs photons from the excitation source and undergoes radiative oscillations causing changes in the CPD@BA dipole distribution. When there is no energy transfer, the excited CPD@BA returns to the ground state and emits green phosphorescence. However, since there is a large area overlap between the phosphorescence spectrum of CPD@BA and the absorption spectrum of RhB, the dipole oscillations of CPD@BA can cause changes in the distribution of neighboring RhB dipoles. When the vibration frequencies of both dipoles are the same, energy transfer occurs between CPD@BA and RhB, thus realizing efficient ultra-broadband three-peak white light emission from CPD-RhB@BA-1.

We further investigated the effects of different receptor RhB concentrations on the phosphorescence properties of CPD-RhB@BA. As shown in Fig. 4 a, by changing the ratios of donor and acceptor, the afterglow intensity ratio (600 nm/530 nm) of CPD-RhB@BA composites increases from 0.43 to 3.646. The afterglow color of CPD-RhB@BA composites changes from green, yellow, and orange to red. And the afterglow decay at 530 nm shortens from 1.711 to 0.673 s (Fig. 4b and Table S1), demonstrating that an efficient FRET happens between the donor and acceptor. With the ratio of donor and acceptor increases, the distance between the donor and acceptor decreases. This leads to a gradual increase in energy transfer efficiency and the highest energy transfer efficiency can reach 99%.

The ultra-broad spectral characteristics of CPD-RhB@BA make it very advantageous in constituting high-performance white LEDs. We chose to mix the epoxy resin A glue and B glue in a certain ratio and added CPD-RhB@BA-1, and coated them directly onto the surface of a commercial 395 nm UV-LED chip after natural drying was completed to prepare white LEDs (Fig. 5 a). The FL spectra of high-efficiency white LEDs fabricated based on CPD-RhB@BA phosphors covers the whole visible region, and three different peaks can be clearly observed (Fig. 5b): they correspond to blue fluorescence, green phosphorescence and red-light emission realized based on FERT, respectively. The white LEDs have a sufficient red component with CIE coordinates of (0.35,0.31) and a CCT value of 4041K (Fig. 5c). In addition, multicolor LEDs were prepared by selecting CPD-RhB@BA composites prepared with different donor and acceptor ratios as phosphors (Fig. 5d), which greatly expanded the application prospects of multicolor CPDs. These results demonstrate the excellent potential of phosphors based on CPDs in the field of LEDs.

In summary, we using high-efficiency blue fluorescence and green phosphorescent dual emission CPD@BA as the energy donor and RhB with strong red fluorescence emission as the energy acceptor, successfully synthesized CPD-RhB@BA composites with three emission peaks (blue, green and red) assisted FRET white emission system. CPD-RhB@BA-1 showed bright white light under 395 nm excitation. The QY value is as high as 36%, and the spectral FWHM reached 235 nm. In addition, multicolor CPDs materials with afterglow colors ranging from green to red were prepared by varying the acceptor content. Preparation of WLEDs using CPD-RhB@BA-1 as phosphor and the electroluminescence (EL) spectra showed unique triple-peak emission under the excitation of the UV-LED chip, CIE and CCT values reached (0.35, 0.31) and 4041 K, respectively. This work provides a feasible method for the synthesis of white light emitting materials.

Jing Tan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Dengke Cheng: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hailing Gu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Daiqi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Chi Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Yi Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Hongjia Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Longtao Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Chunyi Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Qijun Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu (BK20231537), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023M732973).
